paragraphs.text: An almost-forgotten story of an early day female Robinson Crusoe who for 18 years braved the loneliness of rock-bound San Nicholas island off Santa Barbara's coast was revived yesterday with the return of an expedition from the Channel islands.

Arthur Woodward, director of history and anthropology of the Los Angeles museum, said there the expedition apparently located the site of the hut occupied by the Indian woman, who later was baptized at the Santa Barbara Old Mission as Juana Maria.

The woman was left alone on the Island in 1835, by an expedition that had been sent from Santa Barbara to remove the Indians from the place. Just as the boat was being loaded with Indians and a storm approached, Juana Maria ran from the gathering, scampered up the hills, and cried she had forgotten her baby.

The expedition was forced to depart because of the storm, and Juana Maria was soon forgotten. For 18 years she lived alone on the island, Woodward said. Her baby died meanwhile. The woman lived by fishing and hunting with snares.

In 1853, an expedition headed by George Nidever of Santa Barbara visited the island and captured the half-wild woman, who could speak the language only of her tribe, all of whom were then dead. She died six weeks later and was buried at the Santa Barbara mission.

Woodward said the expedition returning today also had found what appeared to be the "mint" of early-day California. On Santa Cruz and Santa Rosa islands, he said, he found 1,700 fine stone drills used to make beads, which served as the currency for the early day Indians.
Santa Rosa, Santa Cruz, San Nicholas and San Miguel islands were visited during which nearly 200 village sites were inspected and from which relics were obtained, Woodward said.
This ever fascinating story of Juana Maria is to be told and portions of it dramatized over KTMS at 7 o'clock tonight.
